def generate_signals(df, params):
    import pandas as pd
    import numpy as np
    import ta
    
    # Parameters
    lookback = params['lookback']
    entry_z = params['entry_z']
    exit_z = params['exit_z']
    
    # Calculate daily returns
    close = df['close']
    daily_returns = close.pct_change()
    
    # Volatility estimate (standard deviation of returns)
    vol = daily_returns.rolling(window=lookback).std()
    
    # Z-score of volatility relative to its own history
    vol_mean = vol.rolling(window=lookback*2).mean()
    vol_std = vol.rolling(window=lookback*2).std()
    vol_z = (vol - vol_mean) / vol_std
    
    # Entry: when vol is extremely high (panic), go long; when extremely low (complacency), go short
    # Exit: when vol reverts closer to mean
    signals = pd.Series(0, index=df.index)
    signals[vol_z > entry_z] = 1      # high vol -> long (mean reversion up)
    signals[vol_z < -entry_z] = -1     # low vol -> short (mean reversion down)
    
    # Exit condition: vol_z returns to neutral zone
    exit_mask = (vol_z.abs() < exit_z)
    signals[exit_mask] = 0
    
    # Forward fill: stay in position until exit triggers
    # But we must not look ahead; we use a simple loop to carry forward signals
    position = 0
    final_signals = pd.Series(0, index=df.index)
    for i in range(len(df)):
        if signals.iloc[i] != 0:
            position = signals.iloc[i]
        # If exit condition met, reset to 0
        if exit_mask.iloc[i]:
            position = 0
        final_signals.iloc[i] = position
    
    return final_signals.astype(int)
rationale: Ethereum exhibits volatility clustering and mean reversion in volatility itself; extreme volatility spikes (panic) tend to reverse upward as markets normalize, while extremely low volatility (complacency) often precedes downward moves, allowing a risk-parity style entry based on volatility regime.
